title: PROJECT_ROOT and TEMP_ROOT LazyLocks silently fall back to '.' if cwd canonicalize fails
description:
## Severity
Major

## Dimension
Perimeter Defense / Functional Correctness

## Location
src/tools/file/mod.rs:78-88 (PROJECT_ROOT and TEMP_ROOT static initializers)

## Violation
```rust
static PROJECT_ROOT: LazyLock<PathBuf> = LazyLock::new(|| {
    std::env::current_dir()
        .and_then(|cwd| cwd.canonicalize())
        .unwrap_or_else(|_| PathBuf::from("."))
});
```

If `current_dir` or `canonicalize` fails (e.g. the process was started in a deleted directory, or the cwd is mounted via FUSE that returns EIO), PROJECT_ROOT silently falls back to `PathBuf::from(".")` — a *relative* path. Every subsequent jail check using `path_is_within(&canonical, &PROJECT_ROOT)` compares a fully-canonicalized absolute path against `.`. `Path::starts_with` is byte-prefix matching: `"/etc/passwd".starts_with(".")` is false BUT also `"/home/user/file"`.starts_with(".") is false — so EVERY path is rejected, breaking the entire tool subsystem silently.

Worse, even if canonicalize succeeds, no startup-time check verifies that PROJECT_ROOT actually exists OR that it's a directory (a path that resolves to a regular file would still pass the canonicalize check). The "jail" is built on assumptions that aren't enforced.

## Mandated refactor
1. Make PROJECT_ROOT and TEMP_ROOT typed as `Result<PathBuf, anyhow::Error>` (or wrap in LazyLock<Result<...>>). At first use, propagate the error to the caller rather than substituting `.`.
2. In main.rs startup, eagerly access both statics and PANIC with a clear error message ("could not determine project root: ..."). Fail fast at process start, not silently per-tool-call.
3. Reject PROJECT_ROOT that is not a directory: `assert!(canonical.is_dir())`.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
